scenario_name: Scenario 19 — Spec Conflicts with Existing Behavior
justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior identified as potentially impacted — explicitly names both tiers. (2) Escalation quantifies what would change: premium drops from 20% to 0% unless new-customer, gold drops from 15% similarly. (3) Three specific clarifying options presented (full replace, new-first with tier fallback, tier-first with new for non-tier) mapping to the rubric's preserve/precedence/replace questions. (4) No implementation provided — explicitly refuses to write code until clarified. (5) Escalation is clearly formatted and directed to Bird as domain authority.

observations.text: Correctly identifies the core conflict: spec is silent on tier customers, not just incomplete
Quantifies the risk: premium customers would lose 20% discount, gold would lose 15%
Presents three distinct resolution options (A/B/C) that map cleanly to the rubric's three clarifying questions
Refuses to implement until clarified — no silent regression possible
Correctly routes escalation to Bird as domain authority rather than trying to resolve it alone
Strong framing: 'The cost of asking is one turn. The cost of removing 20% discounts silently could be much higher'

agent_output_excerpt:
**ESCALATION — Cannot safely implement without clarification.**

Before writing a single line, I need to flag a critical ambiguity in this spec.

---

## The Problem

The new spec (AC1–AC3) is built entirely around **account age and purchase recency**. The existing function is built entirely around **customer tier (premium/gold)**. The spec explicitly notes that premium and gold are not mentioned — but it doesn't say what to do with them.

There are three meaningfully different interpretations:
